html:
Allah uncovers His Shin — believers prostrate; hypocrites turn to stone Allah's Character Cosmology Strong Sahih Muslim #183
"Our Lord will uncover His Shin, and all believers, male and female, will prostrate themselves before Him. But there will remain those who used to prostrate only to be seen — they will try, but their backs will become like a single plate."

What the hadith says

On Judgment Day, Allah will uncover His Shin as a sign. True believers will prostrate spontaneously; hypocrites — who prayed only to be seen — will find their backs rigid, unable to bow.

Why this is a problem

  1. Direct contradiction with Q 42:11 ("nothing is like Him"). Allah revealing a body part implies He has a body. Classical theology insists on divine transcendence while preserving this anthropomorphic hadith.
  2. No theological consensus has emerged after 1,400 years of debate. Classical theologians argued for centuries over whether to accept the shin literally (anthropomorphists), without asking how (bila kayf), or to interpret it figuratively (Mu'tazila, later Ash'arites). No resolution was reached.

The Muslim response

The dominant classical Sunni position is bila kayf — accepting the attribute without asking about its modality. We affirm Allah has a Shin in a manner befitting His majesty, without comparing it to human anatomy. This preserves the text without committing to anthropomorphism.

Why it fails

A Judgment Day climax that hinges on a body part Allah is simultaneously said not to have is a Judgment Day scripted by people who had not yet reconciled their own theology. Bila kayf is a deferral, not a resolution — it concedes that the hadith and Q 42:11 cannot be coherently combined, and asks the believer to hold the tension without resolving it. A tradition whose central eschatological vision requires supernatural epistemic suspension to avoid contradiction has preserved its incoherence rather than resolved it.

"A people that entrusts its affairs to a woman will not prosper" Governance Women Strong Bukhari 6834; cross-referenced in Muslim's governance-era parallel
"Never will a people who entrust their affair to a woman succeed."

What the hadith says

Muhammad's response on hearing the Persians had enthroned a queen — preserved as a categorical rule against female leadership in classical Islamic jurisprudence.

Why this is a problem

  1. A universal prophetic rule derived from one historical event. The hadith generalises from one comment about one foreign queen to a comprehensive prohibition on female political leadership.
  2. Used to block Muslim women from political office across 1,400 years. Classical Sunni jurisprudence uses this hadith to prohibit women from serving as heads of state, judges, and in other senior roles.
  3. Empirically falsified — repeatedly. Benazir Bhutto (Pakistan), Sheikh Hasina (Bangladesh), and Khaleda Zia (Bangladesh) led Muslim-majority states successfully. The prophesied ruin did not materialise.

The Muslim response

"The hadith applies to the leadership structures of the time; modern governance is different from 7th-century Persian monarchy. Scholars debate whether the prohibition applies to elected democratic leaders." Some reformist scholars argue the hadith was contextual, not universal.

Why it fails

If the hadith was contextual, the classical jurists who used it to bar women from leadership for 1,400 years were wrong — and the tradition should say so. Instead, the contextual reading is a modern retreat from a ruling the tradition enforced universally until empirical evidence made it untenable. A prophetic rule that modern Muslim-majority states have functionally abandoned because female-led governments did not fail is a rule whose predictive content has been refuted. Its only remaining power is the weight of tradition — not the weight of evidence.

Ten sucklings reduced to five — and both versions were recited as Quran at Muhammad's death Abrogation Scripture Integrity Strong Sahih Muslim #1452
"There was revealed in the Quran: 'Ten definite sucklings make marriage unlawful.' Then it was abrogated by: 'Five definite sucklings.' When the Messenger of Allah died, it was among what was recited in the Quran."

What the hadith says

Two different "Quranic" verses about breastfeeding kinship existed — one replacing the other — and Aisha attests both were still in recitation at the Prophet's death. Neither appears in today's Quran.

Why this is a problem

  1. An abrogated verse remained in recitation after its cancellation. The five-sucklings verse was still being recited as Quran even after it was supposed to have replaced the ten-sucklings verse.
  2. Neither verse appears in today's Quran — so at least one "Quran verse" was removed after the Prophet's death. Aisha is explicit: the verse was still in recitation when the Prophet died. Its absence from the current Quran means it disappeared after his death.
  3. Shatters the claim that nothing has been added or removed. The preservation doctrine (Q 15:9) is directly contradicted by the Prophet's own widow testifying that recited Quranic verses were lost.

The Muslim response

Naskh al-tilawa duna al-hukm — the wording was abrogated, the ruling preserved. Allah's wisdom removed the text while keeping the legal ruling active. The preservation of "the Quran" refers to its essential message, not every recited syllable.

Why it fails

Q 15:9 says "We will be its guardian" — referring to the Quran as a text. "Essential message preservation" is not what the verse claims; textual preservation is. Aisha's testimony places the disappearance of a recited verse after the Prophet's death — which means the divine guardian permitted a verse to be lost from the text after the Prophet died. The naskh al-tilawa doctrine was developed precisely to absorb this kind of testimony. It works as a rescue mechanism, but only by conceding everything critics claim: the current Quran is missing revelation that was once in it, and this is compatible with divine preservation only by redefining what "preserved" means.

The Quran was revealed in "seven letters" — no one agrees what those are Scripture Integrity Contradictions Strong Sahih Muslim #819, #820
"This Quran has been revealed in seven ways (ahruf), so recite according to whichever is easiest."

What the hadith says

The Quran is declared to have seven legitimate recitation forms (sab'at ahruf). Classical scholars produced 35+ competing definitions of what "seven" means and could not reach consensus.

Why this is a problem

  1. Seven different readings means there is no single authoritative Quran. Yet the Quran claims to be one perfectly preserved book. Seven valid readings — some differing in meaning, not just pronunciation — undermine this claim.
  2. Modern qira'at sometimes differ in meaning. The ten canonical readings include variants that change the sense of a verse — "they will kill" versus "they will be killed" in the same passage. These are not trivial pronunciation differences.
  3. ʿUthman burned variant manuscripts precisely because the "seven letters" were producing conflict. The burning of Quranic manuscripts under the third caliph — acknowledged in Islamic history — was necessary because the variants were causing theological disputes. Unity was built by suppressing the plurality.

The Muslim response

"The seven ahruf refer to permitted linguistic variations (dialect, pronunciation, word-choice within the same meaning) — not different Qurans. The ten canonical qira'at are all traceable to the Prophet and all convey the same essential meaning."

Why it fails

The claim that all qira'at convey "the same essential meaning" is empirically false for some variants where the difference is substantive, not merely phonological. And 35+ competing classical definitions of what "seven ahruf" means — with no resolution — shows that the tradition itself does not know what the hadith means. A scripture declared revealed in seven forms but then policed into uniformity by burning the other variants is a scripture whose unity was built by silencing the other versions. The burning of manuscripts is not a detail to footnote; it is the mechanism by which the "single preserved Quran" was constructed.

Charity after death benefits the dead — contradicting "no soul bears another's burden" Contradictions Logical Inconsistency Strong Sahih Muslim #1631; Q 53:38–39
"When a person dies, all his deeds come to an end, except three: continuing charity, useful knowledge, and a righteous child who prays for him."

What the hadith says

Three ongoing contributions can earn a dead person reward in the grave. But Quran 53:38–39 insists no person bears another's burden, and man only gets what he strives for.

Why this is a problem

  1. "A righteous child prays for him" = one soul's merit transferred to another — directly contradicting Q 53:39: "man will not have except what he strived for." A dead person receiving benefit from a child's prayer is precisely the cross-soul merit transfer the Quran prohibits.
  2. Produces a religious marketplace for post-death prayer services. The hadith licensed an entire economy of intercessory prayer, charitable endowments in the deceased's name, and professional recitation of Quran for the dead — all contrary to the Quran's own economy of merit.

The Muslim response

"The hadith and Q 53:39 address different categories: Q 53:39 concerns individual accountability on Judgment Day; the hadith concerns ongoing benefit from deeds the person set in motion during their lifetime. A child praying is a fruit of the parent's righteous upbringing — still the parent's own striving, expressed through the child."

Why it fails

The harmonisation requires Q 53:39 ("man gets what he strives for") to mean "man gets what he set in motion" — a different claim. And a third party's post-mortem prayer for the deceased is not the same as the deceased's own striving expressed through the child. The hadith's plain content — a righteous child's prayer benefits a dead person — is cross-soul merit transfer. A theology that preserves an ethical claim (individual accountability) and then disables it with a soft-merit loophole has built into itself the exact commerce in salvation it originally denounced.

Adam won a theological argument with Moses — because "it was written before he was created" Logical Inconsistency Contradictions Strong Sahih Muslim #2652
"Moses said to Adam: 'You are the one whose sin expelled humanity from paradise.' Adam replied: 'Are you blaming me for an act which was written for me before I was created?' So Adam refuted Moses." — And the Prophet confirmed Adam won.

What the hadith says

In a Muhammad-narrated debate between Adam and Moses, Adam invokes predestination as his defense — and is declared the winner. "It was written before I was created" is affirmed as a valid exculpatory argument by the Prophet himself.

Why this is a problem

  1. Explicitly endorses the defense "I was predestined to sin, so don't blame me" as valid. If it works for Adam, the logic applies to every sinner: "It was written before I was created."
  2. If this argument works for Adam, it works for everyone — yet the religion still hands out hellfire for disbelief. The tradition simultaneously affirms predestination (Adam wins the argument) and moral accountability (people burn in hell for their choices). These positions are not compatible.

The Muslim response

Classical theology reads the hadith as establishing divine predestination (qadar) without licensing human fatalism. Adam's victory is on a metaphysical point: Allah's foreknowledge preceded his act. The Ash'arite khalq/kasb distinction resolves the apparent contradiction: Allah creates the act; the human acquires responsibility for it.

Why it fails

The Ash'arite compatibilism is the theological scaffolding developed precisely to manage this contradiction — and its opacity is proverbial. Adam's argument in the hadith is structurally the defense of every sinner: "I was written that way." If the defense works for the first human, the scripture has licensed it in principle for every human. The religion still hands out eternal punishment for disbelief — which is inconsistent with accepting Adam's defense. Either foreknowledge plus creation renders the sinner unfree (in which case hell is unjust), or the sinner is free and Adam's argument should fail (in which case the hadith is wrong). The tradition has tried to have both; the hadith records the cost of that attempt.

The sun goes and prostrates beneath Allah's throne — then asks permission to rise Cosmology Strange / Obscure Strong Sahih Muslim #159
"The sun goes down and prostrates under the Throne, and seeks permission to rise. When the time comes to order her to rise from the west, she will not receive permission."

What the hadith says

The sun is a sentient creature that bows daily under Allah's throne and asks permission to rise each morning. At the end of time it will be denied permission and will rise from the west instead.

Why this is a problem

  1. The sun is a hydrogen-fusion star 150 million km away, not a personified courtier orbiting an empyrean throne. What we experience as sunset is Earth rotating, not the sun travelling under the earth to prostrate.
  2. Treats celestial mechanics as a daily bureaucratic process. Allah's throne has a spatial location below which the sun physically rests; this is flat-earth-adjacent cosmology, not metaphor.
  3. The end-times implication — sun rising from the west — requires Earth's rotation to reverse. This is not cosmological poetry; it is a specific physical claim that is physically impossible without catastrophic consequences.

The Muslim response

"The sun's prostration is a spiritual reality we cannot directly observe — not a literal physical journey. The hadith uses anthropomorphic language to convey the sun's submission to Allah's sovereignty." Modern concordist rescue: the sun does travel on a path through the galaxy, so "traveling to a resting place" is technically compatible with stellar motion.

Why it fails

The hadith specifies a mustaqarr (resting place) — a spatial term for a physical location under the Throne. Spiritualising the sun's prostration while preserving its spatial location is incoherent. The concordist galaxy-motion rescue simply matches any motion to any language after the fact — the sun's galactic orbit has no relation to Islamic end-times theology. The classical tradition read this account literally for 1,200 years; the modern rescues are responses to scientific embarrassment, not exegesis.

Hell is brought on Judgment Day with seventy thousand reins, each pulled by 70,000 angels Hell Strange / Obscure Strong Sahih Muslim #2842
"Hell will be brought that Day with seventy thousand reins — each rein held by seventy thousand angels."

What the hadith says

Hell is a creature-like entity restrained by 4.9 billion angels holding chains, dragged into the place of judgment on Judgment Day.

Why this is a problem

  1. Hell is personified as a fighting animal requiring physical restraint — inconsistent with Hell as a created place of torment rather than an autonomous agent.
  2. The numbers are cosmic-scale but apply to an immaterial concept. 4.9 billion angels holding 70,000 reins on an entity that must be physically restrained transforms eschatological theology into mythological spectacle.

The Muslim response

"The imagery conveys Hell's immensity, the power required to contain it, and the dramatic scale of Judgment Day. It is eschatological metaphor emphasising Allah's absolute control even over the instrument of punishment."

Why it fails

Classical Sunni eschatology read this literally — al-Tirmidhi's hadith commentaries and Ibn Kathir's eschatological writings treat Hell's physical arrival in chains as a real event. The "metaphor" move is modern. More importantly: if Hell must be physically restrained from rampaging, the hadith implies Hell has autonomous destructive will — which creates the theologically embarrassing image of Allah needing 4.9 billion angels to hold back a creation He made. A hell so enormous it must be walked in on billions of angelic leashes has converted an abstract ethical category into a Bronze-Age monster parade.

"He who copies any people is one of them" — the tashabbuh hadith and cultural quarantine Disbelievers Governance Moral Problems Internal Contradictions Logical Inconsistency Ritual Absurdities Strong Abu Dawud #4032
"The Messenger of Allah said: man tashabbaha bi-qawmin fa-huwa minhum — He who copies any people is one of them." (Abu Dawud #4032)

What the hadith says

Whoever deliberately makes himself similar to a people (qawm) becomes one of them. Ibn Taymiyyah's Iqtida' al-Sirat al-Mustaqim built this into a comprehensive system prohibiting Muslims from imitating non-Muslims in clothing, festivals, grooming, food-styles, and cultural practices. Modern Salafi fatawa deploy it against Christmas celebrations, neckties, birthday cakes, Valentine's Day, and specific hairstyles.

Why this is a problem

  1. The hadith encodes group-identity-as-soteriology. Deliberate outward imitation does not merely associate you with a group — it makes you a member of that group. A Muslim wearing a Christmas sweater has, on the plain-text reading, become "one of" the Christians with the soteriological consequences that carries.
  2. It directly conflicts with Q 49:13 — "We made you peoples and tribes that you may know one another." Mutual acquaintance (ta'aruf) is impossible if every group must keep its outward forms hermetically distinct from every other group. The hadith's quarantine principle reduces Q 49:13 to a near-empty claim.
  3. The doctrine has no limiting principle — "qawm" is unspecified. The text says "whoever imitates a people" with no specification of which people. Classical jurists supplied the gloss "non-Muslims" — but the text equally applies to Sunnis imitating Shia, Arabs imitating South Asian Muslims, or any Muslim imitating any other group. Ibn Taymiyyah's systematic extension confirms the text's open scope.
  4. Muhammad himself imitated non-Muslim forms strategically. He adopted the Roman diplomatic seal-ring; used Persian trench-warfare techniques; wore Yemeni and Egyptian textiles. These adoptions are praised in the tradition. The doctrine applies restrictively to ordinary believers but not to the Prophet — revealing that the principle is juristic discretion, not a universal rule.

The Muslim response

Classical and modern apologetics distinguish religious imitation (wearing a cross as a religious symbol) from religiously-neutral cultural overlap (wearing a tie because it is office dress code). The prohibition targets deliberate identity-claiming imitation, not incidental form-similarity. The hadith's purpose is pastoral protection of community cohesion, especially for minorities at risk of assimilation.

Why it fails

The religious-vs-cultural distinction is not in the hadith text — it is a post-hoc juristic limitation applied to an unqualified statement. Ibn Taymiyyah's expansion to culturally-neutral forms (calendars, festivals, food-styles) shows the text's plain scope supports the broader application. The "weak imitation is fine" position is held by some moderate jurists but is not the dominant classical reading. The community-protection framing is historically plausible for 7th-century Medina — but universalising a minority-protection rule across all contexts for 1,400 centuries has produced the Saudi religious police, Taliban dress-regulation, and widespread low-grade religious scrupulosity around cultural participation. A rule evaluated by how communities actually deploy it has produced a regulatory technology of cultural-border policing that the apologetic's idealized reading cannot account for.

Muhammad ordered the assassination of Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf by deception — one hadith later, he forbade assassination Prophetic Character Violence Moral Problems Internal Contradictions Governance Strong Abu Dawud #2769; #2770
"The Messenger of Allah said: 'Who will pursue Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf, for he has caused trouble to Allah and His Apostle?' Muhammad ibn Maslamah stood up and said: 'I shall do it, Messenger of Allah... Do you want me to kill him?' He said: 'Yes.' He said: 'Then permit me to say something [false against you].' He said: 'Yes, say it.'... So they struck him until they killed him." (#2769)

"The Prophet said: 'Faith has prevented assassination. A believer should not assassinate.'" (#2770)

What the hadith says

Abu Dawud #2769: Muhammad commissions the assassination of Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf — a Jewish poet who insulted him after Badr — and explicitly permits the assassin to lie about Muhammad to gain Ka'b's trust. The operation unfolds through layers of deception: a false complaint, a fabricated loan request, hair-touching to establish physical control, then death. #2770, one hadith later in the same book: "Faith prevented assassination; a believer should not assassinate."

Why this is a problem

  1. Muhammad explicitly permitted lying — including slander of himself — as an operational method. "Permit me to say something false" + "Yes, say it" is a blanket pre-authorisation for deception in a killing operation. Classical commentary (al-Nawawi, Ibn Hajar) confirmed the permission as a war-necessity exception.
  2. Abu Dawud placed the absolute prohibition against assassination one hadith after the concrete commission — an unresolved canonical self-contradiction. Both appear in the same Kitab al-Jihad chapter cluster. The compiler preserved the tension without resolution.
  3. The killing method — exploiting hospitality and trust to achieve physical control — is precisely what the Arabic fatk (treacherous killing) describes. Ka'b came out in trust; he was killed by men who had ingratiated themselves through deception. Ibn Manzur's Lisan al-'Arab defines fatk as "killing by treachery against a person who has no suspicion" — which is exactly what happened.
  4. The Ka'b precedent entered all four classical Sunni law schools as foundational Prophetic sunnah for covert targeted killing. It has been cited from the Rushdie fatwa to the Charlie Hebdo murders as canonical authority for killing critics of Islam.

The Muslim response

Three tracks: (1) Ka'b was an enemy combatant who had personally incited the Quraysh to renewed war after Badr — a military-political operative, not a civilian satirist. (2) The permission to lie was a war-necessity (darura) exception, not a general warrant. (3) The fatk-prohibition at #2770 addresses peacetime protected relationships, not active enemy-combatants.

Why it fails

The canonical charge against Ka'b is that he "caused trouble to Allah and His Apostle" — a speech-and-poetry charge. The military-conspirator framing is developed in later biographical sources; the hadith itself operates on a speech-offense charge. If causing trouble through poetry makes someone a legitimate assassination target, the category extends to every critic and satirist — which is exactly how the precedent has been used. The darura exception requires emergency necessity with no alternative; the operation was planned in advance with scripted lies, not an emergency requiring ad hoc deception. And the Arabic fatk describes the Ka'b killing precisely — a person killed by trusted visitors with no suspicion. Abu Dawud placed both hadiths in the same chapter knowing the tension; the canonical record preserves the contradiction, and the tradition has resolved it by applying the commission as operative sunnah while treating the prohibition as a general principle with Ka'b-based exceptions.

"Kill those who change their religion" — Abu Dawud Book 40 preserves the unconditional death-sentence for apostasy Apostasy & Blasphemy Moral Problems Governance Hudud Internal Contradictions Strong Abu Dawud #4353; #4356; #4358
"'Ali burned some people who retreated from Islam. When Ibn 'Abbas was informed of it, he said: 'If it had been I, I would not have burned them, for the Messenger of Allah said: Do not inflict Allah's punishment on anyone, but would have killed them on account of the statement of the Messenger of Allah. The Apostle said: Kill those who change their religion (man baddala dinahu faqtuluhu).'"

(#4356): "Mu'adh said: I will not sit until he is killed according to the decision of Allah and His Apostle. He said it three times. He then commanded for it and he was killed."

What the hadith says

Abu Dawud Book 40 preserves a tight cluster establishing death as the canonical Islamic penalty for apostasy. The anchor text is "man baddala dinahu faqtuluhu" — "whoever changes his religion, kill him" — cited by Ibn ʿAbbas and confirmed by ʿAli. The Yemen case law at #4356–4357 shows Muʿadh ibn Jabal refusing to sit or dismount until a Yemeni Jewish convert who had reverted to Judaism was executed — based solely on religious reversion, with no armed rebellion alleged.

Why this is a problem

  1. The text is unconditional. "Man baddala dinahu faqtuluhu" — universal subject, no qualifier, imperative result. No armed-rebellion requirement appears in the text. The Yemen case law confirms the unconditional reading: the executed man's only stated offense was religious reversion.
  2. ʿAli and Ibn ʿAbbas disagreed about method, not penalty. Both senior Companions — the fourth Caliph and the canonical Quran commentator — treated execution as mandatory. The dispute was procedural, not substantive. No founding-generation voice argued against the death penalty itself.
  3. The ruling operates as active state law today. Saudi Arabia, Iran, Afghanistan, Mauritania, Yemen, Qatar, and parts of Nigeria apply death or severe punishment for apostasy. The canonical text is the jurisprudential foundation.
  4. Direct tension with Q 2:256 — "there is no compulsion in religion." If there is no compulsion in religion, Muslims should be free to leave without state enforcement. Classical Sunni jurisprudence restricts Q 2:256 to initial adoption but not departure — a restriction the verse itself does not state.

The Muslim response

Three tracks: (1) The political-rebellion limitation — the penalty applies to apostasy combined with armed insurgency, not private belief change. (2) The classical-suspension argument — the death penalty is theoretical maximum that legitimate Islamic governance should not implement; the repentance offer is the operative procedure. (3) The Quranic-primacy argument — Q 2:256 and 18:29 establish religious freedom as the Quranic principle, and the hadith must be read through that framework.

Why it fails

The political-rebellion limitation is not in the canonical text, and the Yemen case law at #4356–4357 delivers the decisive counter-example: Muʿadh's execution order was immediate, based solely on religious reversion, with no mention of armed rebellion. The classical-suspension argument concedes the point — if the death penalty should not be implemented, the apologist is arguing Islamic governance should override the canonical text, not that the canonical text says what the apologist claims. The Quranic-primacy argument is the most honest reformist position, but it requires prioritising Q 2:256 over a sahih hadith preserved across all six canonical Sunni collections — a methodological move classical Sunni usul al-fiqh explicitly prohibits. Modern Muslim moral progress on apostasy requires overriding a direct, sahih, multi-collection Prophetic dictum. That is the honest statement of the problem.

"Whoever acquires knowledge of astrology acquires a branch of magic" — star-knowledge classified as sorcery Science Internal Contradictions Strange Theology Logic Strong Abu Dawud #3906
"The Prophet said: 'If anyone acquires any knowledge of astrology, he acquires a branch of magic of which he gets more as long as he continues to do so.'"

What the hadith says

Muhammad classifies 'ilm al-nujum ("knowledge of the stars" — covering both astrology and astronomy in classical Arabic) as sihr (magic/sorcery): acquiring star-knowledge is acquiring a branch of magic, and the more one studies the more magical the knowledge becomes.

Why this is a problem

  1. The classical Islamic Golden Age of astronomy occurred under religious-institutional patronage. The Abbasid Bayt al-Hikmah produced al-Battani, al-Tusi, and Ibn al-Shatir — major astronomical contributions made while this hadith was canonical. The hadith's blanket condemnation of star-knowledge creates structural tension with Islam's most-celebrated scientific achievements.
  2. Classical Sunni jurisprudence had to develop the astrology-vs-astronomy distinction post-Prophetically — because the hadith does not make it. The text classifies all 'ilm al-nujum as sihr without qualification. Jurists had to supply the distinction to allow prayer-time calculation and qibla-determination.
  3. The "the more, the more" structure is theologically peculiar. Increased astronomical knowledge produces increased magical-correspondence — as if studying the stars literally increases one's magical contamination. Modern Saudi religious authorities have invoked this hadith to oppose mathematical astronomy in moon-sighting contexts.

The Muslim response

Classical Sunni apologetic distinguishes positional astronomy (tasyiriyy — observation for prayer-times and qibla) from predictive astrology (ta'thiriyy — using stars to forecast earthly events). The hadith condemns the latter only; the former is permitted because it serves religious-functional purposes. The Islamic Golden Age astronomers framed their work within the permitted category.

Why it fails

The astrology-vs-astronomy distinction is post-Prophetic jurisprudential development; the canonical hadith classifies all 'ilm al-nujum as sihr without the distinction. Classical-Arabic 'ilm al-nujum covered both observational and predictive stellar knowledge; the distinction the apologetic relies on requires semantic development that the hadith itself does not declare. The fact that classical Islamic astronomers had to explicitly defend their work against accusations of forbidden astrology shows the distinction was not stable. Modern Saudi opposition to mathematical astronomy in moon-sighting is the operational evidence that the canonical hadith's plain reading remains live in some classical-Salafi circles. The text says what it says: stellar knowledge is a branch of magic, and the more you study, the more magical the knowledge.
